sections.label: Section 281
sections.original: ἔγνως · λόγου δὲ δὶς τόσου μʼ ἐκούφισας .
sections.translation: You have it ; and lighten me of half my speech .

sections.label: Section 282
sections.original: καὶ πῶς πρὸς Ἴδης ὀργάδας πορεύεται ,
sections.translation: How is it that he comes to Ida ’ s meadows , wandering from the broad wagon track across the plain ?

sections.label: Section 284
sections.original: οὐκ οἶδʼ ἀκριβῶς · εἰκάσαι γε μὴν πάρα .
sections.translation: I cannot say for certain , though I might guess .

sections.label: Section 285
sections.original: νυκτὸς γὰρ οὔτι φαῦλον ἐμβαλεῖν στρατόν ,
sections.translation: It is no idle task for an army to make an invasion by night , hearing that the plains are packed with foemen ’ s troops . But he frightened us rustic shepherds who dwell along the slopes of Ida , the earliest settlement in the land , as he came by night through the wood full of wild beasts .

sections.label: Section 290
sections.original: πολλῇ γὰρ ἠχῇ Θρῄκιος ῥέων στρατὸς
sections.translation: On surged the tide of Thracian warriors with loud shouts ; at this in wild amazement we drove our flocks unto the heights , for fear that some Argives were coming to plunder and harry your steading , till we caught the sound of voices

sections.label: Section 295
sections.original: ἐδεξάμεσθα καὶ μετέστημεν φόβου .
sections.translation: other than Greek and ceased from our alarm . Then I went and questioned in the Thracian tongue those who were reconnoitring the road for their lord , who it was that lead them , and whose son he was called , that came to the city to help the sons of Priam .

sections.label: Section 300
sections.original: καὶ πάντʼ ἀκούσας ὧν ἐφιέμην μαθεῖν ,
sections.translation: And when I had heard all I wished to learn , I stood still ; and I see Rhesus mounted like a god upon his Thracian chariot . Of gold was the yoke that linked the necks of his horses brighter than the snow ;

sections.label: Section 305
sections.original: πέλτη δʼ ἐπʼ ὤμων χρυσοκολλήτοις τύποις
sections.translation: and on his shoulders flashed his shield with figures welded in gold ; while a gorgon of bronze like that on the aegis of the goddess was bound upon the front of his horses , ringing out its note of fear with many a bell . The number of his army you could not reckon

sections.label: Section 310
sections.original: θέσθαι δυναίμην , ὡς ἄπλατον ἦν ἰδεῖν ,
sections.translation: to an exact sum , for it was beyond one ’ s comprehension ; many knights , many ranks of targeteers , many archers , a great crowd of light-armed troops , arrayed in Thracian garb , to bear them company . Such the man who comes to Troy ’ s assistance ,

sections.label: Section 315
sections.original: ὃν οὔτε φεύγων οὔθʼ ὑποσταθεὶς δορὶ
sections.translation: whom the son of Peleus will never escape , either if he tries to escape or if he meets him spear to spear .

sections.label: Section 317
sections.original: ὅταν πολίταις εὐσταθῶσι δαίμονες ,
sections.translation: Whenever the gods stand by the citizens , the tide of fortune glides with easy flow to a successful goal .

sections.label: Section 319
sections.original: πολλούς , ἐπειδὴ τοὐμὸν εὐτυχεῖ δόρυ
sections.translation: I shall find many friends now that fortune smiles

sections.label: Section 320
sections.original: καὶ Ζεὺς πρὸς ἡμῶν ἐστιν , εὑρήσω φίλους .
sections.translation: upon my warring and Zeus is on my side . But we have no need of those who did not share our toils long since , when Ares , driving all before him , was rending the sails of our ship of state with his tempestuous blast . Rhesus has shown the friendship he then bore to Troy ;

sections.label: Section 325
sections.original: ἥκει γὰρ ἐς δαῖτʼ , οὐ παρὼν κυνηγέταις
sections.translation: for he comes to the feast , although he was not with the hunters when they took the prey , nor did he join his spear with theirs .

sections.label: Section 327
sections.original: ὀρθῶς ἀτίζεις κἀπίμομφος εἶ φίλοις ·
sections.translation: You are right to scorn and blame such friends ; yet welcome those who wish to help the state .

sections.label: Section 329
sections.original: ἀρκοῦμεν οἱ σῴζοντες Ἴλιον πάλαι .
sections.translation: We who have long kept Ilium safe are sufficient .

sections.label: Section 330
sections.original: πέποιθας ἤδη πολεμίους ᾑρηκέναι ;
sections.translation: Are you so sure you have already caught the foe ?

sections.label: Section 331
sections.original: πέποιθα · δείξει τοὐπιὸν σέλας θεοῦ .
sections.translation: I am sure ; tomorrow ’ s light will make that plain .

sections.label: Section 332
sections.original: ὅρα τὸ μέλλον · πόλλʼ ἀναστρέφει θεός .
sections.translation: Beware of what may happen ; often fortune veers about .

sections.label: Section 333
sections.original: μισῶ φίλοισιν ὕστερον βοηδρομεῖν .
sections.translation: I loath the friend who brings his help too late .

sections.label: Section 336
sections.original: ὃ δʼ οὖν , ἐπείπερ ἦλθε , σύμμαχος μὲν οὔ ,
sections.translation: But let him , since he has arrived ,

sections.label: Section 337
sections.original: ξένος δὲ πρὸς τράπεζαν ἡκέτω ξένων ·
sections.translation: come to our table not as an ally but as a guest ;

sections.label: Section 338
sections.original: χάρις γὰρ αὐτῷ Πριαμιδῶν διώλετο .
sections.translation: for the gratitude of Priam ’ s sons is forfeit in his case .

sections.label: Section 334
sections.original: ἄναξ , ἀπωθεῖν συμμάχους ἐπίφθονον .
sections.translation: O prince , to turn away allies earns hatred .

sections.label: Section 335
sections.original: φόβος γένοιτʼ ἂν πολεμίοις ὀφθεὶς μόνον .
sections.translation: His mere appearance would cause a panic among the foe .

sections.label: Section 339
sections.original: σύ τʼ εὖ παραινεῖς καὶ σὺ καιρίως σκοπεῖς .
sections.translation: You counsel rightly ; you too take the proper view .

sections.label: Section 340
sections.original: ὁ χρυσοτευχὴς δʼ οὕνεκʼ ἀγγέλου λόγων
sections.translation: Let Rhesus in his gilded armor join the allies of this land , thanks to the messenger ’ s report . Exit the Messenger .

sections.label: Section 1.342
sections.original: Ἀδράστεια μὲν ἁ Διὸς
sections.translation: May Nemesis , daughter of Zeus , check the word that may offend ; for lo ! I will utter all that it is dear

sections.label: Section 1.345
sections.original: προσφιλές ἐστιν εἰπεῖν .
sections.translation: to my soul to say . You have come , O son of the river god , you have come , welcome in your advent , to the halls of Friendship , since late in time your Pierian mother and Strymon , river with fair bridges ,

sections.label: Section 1.350
sections.original: ρος ποταμὸς πορεύει
sections.translation: are sending you to us .

sections.label: Section 1.351
sections.original: Στρυμών , ὅς ποτε τᾶς μελῳ-
sections.translation: Strymon , who begot you , his strong young son , that day his swirling waters found a refuge in the tuneful Muse ’ s virgin bosom .

sections.label: Section 1.355
sections.original: σύ μοι Ζεὺς ὁ φαναῖος
sections.translation: You are my Zeus , my god of light , as you come driving your dappled horses . Now , O Phrygia , O my country , now may you by God ’ s grace address Zeus the Deliverer !

sections.label: Section 2.360
sections.original: ἆρά ποτʼ αὖθις ἁ παλαιὰ Τροΐα
sections.translation: Shall old Troy once more at last spend the whole day in drinking toasts and singing love ’ s praise , while the bewildering wine-cup sends a capacious challenge round ,

sections.label: Section 2.365
sections.original: λαις κατὰ πόντον Ἀτρειδᾶν
sections.translation: as over the sea for Sparta the sons of Atreus quit the Ilian strand ? O friend , with your arm and spear may you do me this service , then safe return .

sections.label: Section 2.370
sections.original: ἐλθέ , φάνηθι , τὰν ζάχρυσον προβαλοῦ
sections.translation: Come , appear , brandish that shield of gold full in Achilles ’ face ; raise it aslant along the chariot ’ s branching rail , urging on your horses , and shaking your lance with double point .

sections.label: Section 2.375
sections.original: λων . σὲ γὰρ οὔτις ὑποστὰς
sections.translation: For none after facing you will ever join the dance on the plains of Argive Hera ; no , but he shall die , slain by Thracians , and this land shall bear the burden of his corpse and be glad .

sections.label: Section 2.379
sections.original: φίλτατον ἄχθος οἴσει .
sections.translation: Hail , all hail ! O mighty prince !

sections.label: Section 380
sections.original: — ἰὼ ἰώ , μέγας ὦ βασιλεῦ .
sections.translation: fair the cub you have bred , 0 Thrace , a ruler in his every look . See his stalwart frame in golden corslet ! Hark to the ringing bells that peal so proudly from his shield-handle .

sections.label: Section 385
sections.original: θεός , ὦ Τροία , θεός , αὐτὸς Ἄρης
sections.translation: A god , O Troy , a god , a very Ares , Strymon ’ s colt and the tuneful Muse ’ s , has come to breathe courage into you .

sections.label: Section 388
sections.original: χαῖρʼ , ἐσθλὸς ἐσθλοῦ παῖς , τύραννε τῆσδε γῆς ,
sections.translation: Brave son of father as brave , Hector , prince of this land , hail ! After many a long day I greet you .

sections.label: Section 390
sections.original: χαίρω δέ σʼ εὐτυχοῦντα καὶ προσήμενον
sections.translation: I rejoice at your success , to see you camped hard on the enemy towers ; I am here to help you raze their walls and fire their fleet of ships .

sections.label: Section 393
sections.original: παῖ τῆς μελῳδοῦ μητέρος Μουσῶν μιᾶς
sections.translation: Son of that tuneful mother , one of the Muses , and of Thracian Strymon ’ s river , I love to speak

sections.label: Section 395
sections.original: τἀληθὲς αἰεὶ κοὐ διπλοῦς πέφυκʼ ἀνήρ .
sections.translation: plain truth always ; nature did not give me a double tongue . Long , long ago should you have come and shared the labors of this land , and not allowed Troy for any help of yours to fall overthrown by hostile Argive spears . You can not say it was any want of invitation

sections.label: Section 400
sections.original: οὐκ ἦλθες οὐδʼ ἤμυνας οὐδʼ ἐπεστράφης .
sections.translation: that kept you from coming with your help to visit us . What herald or embassy from Phrygia did not come to you , urgently requiring your aid for our city ? What sumptuous presents did we not send to you ? But you , brother barbarian though you were ,

sections.label: Section 405
sections.original: Ἕλλησιν ἡμᾶς προύπιες τὸ σὸν μέρος .
sections.translation: pledged away to Hellenes us your barbarian brothers , for all the help you gave . Yet it was I with this arm that raised you from your paltry princedom to high lordship over Thrace , when I fell upon the Thracian chieftains face to face around Pangaeum in Paeonia ’ s land

sections.label: Section 410
sections.original: ἔρρηξα πέλτην , σοὶ δὲ δουλώσας λεὼν
sections.translation: and broke their serried ranks , and gave their people up to you enslaved ; but you have trampled on this great favor done you , and come with laggard step to give your aid when friends are in distress . While they , whom no natural tie of kin constrains , have long been here , and some are dead and in their graves

sections.label: Section 415
sections.original: κεῖνται πεσόντες , πίστις οὐ σμικρὰ πόλει ,
sections.translation: beneath the heaped-up cairn , no mean proof of loyalty to the city ; and others in arms and mounted on their chariots , with steadfast soul endure the icy blast and parching heat of the sun , not pledging one another on couches , as you do , in long deep draughts .

sections.label: Section 420
sections.original: ταῦθʼ , ὡς ἂν εἰδῇς Ἕκτορʼ ὄντʼ ἐλεύθερον ,
sections.translation: This is the charge I bring against you and utter to your face , that you may know how frank is Hector ’ s tongue .

sections.label: Section 422
sections.original: τοιοῦτός εἰμι καὐτός , εὐθεῖαν λόγων
sections.translation: I too am just the same ; straight to the point I cut my way ; no shuffling nature is mine . My heart was wrung with sorer anguish than yours

sections.label: Section 425
sections.original: λύπῃ πρὸς ἧπαρ δυσφορῶν ἐτειρόμην ·
sections.translation: at my absence from this land ; I fumed and chafed , but Scythian people , whose borders march with mine , made war on me on the very eve of my departure for Ilium ; I had reached the strand of the Euxine sea , there to transport my Thracian army .

sections.label: Section 430
sections.original: ἔνθʼ αἱματηρὸς πέλανος ἐς γαῖαν Σκύθης
sections.translation: Then my spear poured out over Scythia ’ s land great drops of bloody rain , and Thrace too shared in the mingled slaughter . This then was what chanced to keep me from coming to the land of Troy and joining your standard . But as soon as I had conquered these and taken their children as hostages

sections.label: Section 435
sections.original: τάξας τʼ ἔτειον δασμὸν ἐς δόμους φέρειν ,
sections.translation: and appointed the yearly tribute they should pay my house , I have come , sailing across the sea ’ s mouth , and on foot traversing the other borders of your land — not as you in your jeers at those carousals of my countrymen hint , nor sleeping soft in gilded palaces ,

sections.label: Section 440
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οἷα πόντον Θρῄκιον φυσήματα
sections.translation: but amid the frozen hurricanes that vex the Thracian sea and the Paeonian shores , learning as I lay awake what suffering is , this soldier ’ s cloak my only wrap . True my coming has tarried , but yet I am in time ; ten years already have you been at the fray ,

sections.label: Section 445
sections.original: κοὐδὲν περαίνεις , ἡμέραν δʼ ἐξ ἡμέρας
sections.translation: and accomplished nothing yet ; day in , day out , you fall , throwing the dice of war with Argives . But the light of one day will be enough for me to sack those towers and fall upon their anchored fleet and slay the Achaeans ; and on the next day I will go

sections.label: Section 450
sections.original: πρὸς οἶκον εἶμι , συντεμὼν τοὺς σοὺς πόνους ,
sections.translation: home from Ilium , at one stroke ending all your toil . Let none of you lay hand to spear to lift it , for I , for all my late arrival , will with my lance make utter havoc of those vaunting Achaeans .

sections.label: Section 1.454
sections.original: ἰὼ ἰώ .
sections.translation: Joy , joy !

sections.label: Section 1.455
sections.original: φίλα θροεῖς , φίλος Διόθεν εἶ · μόνον
sections.translation: sweet champion sent by Zeus ! Only may Zeus , throned on high , keep jealousy , resistless foe , from you for your words ! That fleet of ships from Argos never brought ,

sections.label: Section 1.460
sections.original: οὔτε πρίν τινʼ οὔτε νῦν
sections.translation: neither formerly nor now , among all its warriors a braver than you . How I wonder will Achilles , how will Aias stand the onset of your spear ? Oh ! that I might see that day , my prince ,

sections.label: Section 1.465
sections.original: ὅτῳ πολυφόνου
sections.translation: on which you may wreak vengeance on them , gripping your lance in your death-dealing hand !

sections.label: Section 467
sections.original: τοιαῦτα μέν σοι τῆς μακρᾶς ἀπουσίας
sections.translation: Such exploits am I ready to achieve to atone for my long absence ; ( with due submission to Nemesis I say this ); then when we have cleared this city of its foes
